content:
# Deuteronomy 22 — Disagreement Log

**Chapter:** Deuteronomy 22 (30 verses) | **Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Agents compared:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

## vv.1–3 — "hide yourself" (EMET) vs. "ignore" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "hide yourself from them" / "you are not permitted to hide yourself" |
| LOGOS | "ignore them" / "You may not ignore it" |
| PNEUMA | "do not ignore it" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *hithalalemta* = Hithpael of *alam* = "to hide oneself from." EMET renders the literal reflexive idiom. LOGOS/PNEUMA render the functional meaning ("ignore"). Consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA as the more natural English expression.

---

## v.1 — "going astray" (LOGOS) vs. "straying" (EMET) vs. "wandering loose" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the ox of your brother or his sheep straying" |
| LOGOS | "your brother's ox or his sheep going astray" |
| PNEUMA | "your fellow Israelite's ox or sheep wandering loose" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *nidachim* = "driven away, straying." LOGOS: "going astray." EMET: "straying." PNEUMA adds "fellow Israelite's" (interpretive expansion of "brother"). Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.1 — "your brother" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "your fellow Israelite" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "your brother" |
| LOGOS | "your brother" |
| PNEUMA | "your fellow Israelite" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *achikha* = "your brother." PNEUMA's expansion "fellow Israelite" is interpretive. Consensus: "your brother" throughout vv. 1–4.

---

## v.4 — "raise them up" (EMET) vs. "help him to lift them up" (LOGOS) vs. "Help him get it back on its feet" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "you shall certainly raise them up with him" |
| LOGOS | "You shall surely help him to lift them up" |
| PNEUMA | "Help him get it back on its feet" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *haqem taqim immo* = "raising you shall raise with him." EMET: "raise them up." LOGOS: "help him to lift them up." PNEUMA: "get it back on its feet" (too idiomatic). Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.5 — "man's garment" vs. "apparel of a man" vs. "men's clothing"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "A woman shall not put on the apparel of a man" |
| LOGOS | "A woman shall not wear a man's garment" |
| PNEUMA | "A woman must not wear men's clothing" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *keli gever* = "the implement/equipment of a man." LOGOS: "a man's garment." PNEUMA: "men's clothing" (plural). Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.5 — "abomination" vs. "detestable"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "is an abomination to the LORD your God" |
| LOGOS | "is an abomination to the LORD your God" |
| PNEUMA | "is detestable to the LORD your God" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *to'evah* = "abomination." EMET/LOGOS preserve the traditional term. PNEUMA: "detestable." Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

## v.6 — "fledglings" (EMET) vs. "young ones" (LOGOS) vs. "the young" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "fledglings or eggs" |
| LOGOS | "young ones or eggs" |
| PNEUMA | "the young or on the eggs" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *efro chim* = "young ones/fledglings." EMET: "fledglings." LOGOS: "young ones." Both are accurate; "young ones" is slightly more accessible. Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.7 — "send away the mother" (EMET) vs. "let the mother go" (LOGOS) vs. "release the mother" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "You shall certainly send away the mother" |
| LOGOS | "You shall surely let the mother go" |
| PNEUMA | "release the mother" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *shalleach teshalach* = cognate construction "sending you shall send." EMET: "send away." LOGOS: "let go." PNEUMA: "release." Consensus follows EMET ("send the mother away") — the verb *shalach* is specifically "send away" and matches the cognate construction in EMET/LOGOS.

---

## v.9 — "two kinds [kilayim]" (EMET) vs. "two kinds of seed" (LOGOS) vs. "two kinds of seed" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "two kinds [kilayim]" |
| LOGOS | "two kinds of seed" |
| PNEUMA | "two kinds of seed" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *kilayim* = "two kinds/mixed kinds." EMET preserves the term with bracket. LOGOS/PNEUMA supply "of seed" for clarity. Consensus follows LOGOS (without the transliteration bracket).

---

## v.9 — "become holy" (literal) vs. "be forfeited" (LOGOS) vs. "forfeit to the sanctuary" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "become holy [for the sanctuary]" |
| LOGOS | "be forfeited—the crop that you have sown and the yield of the vineyard" |
| PNEUMA | "be forfeit to the sanctuary" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *tiqdash* = "become holy/set apart." EMET's bracketed addition "[for the sanctuary]" clarifies the Hebrew well. LOGOS: "forfeited" (practical outcome). PNEUMA: "forfeit to the sanctuary." Consensus follows LOGOS ("be forfeited") without adding "to the sanctuary."

---

## v.12 — "tassels" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) vs. "tassels [gedilim]" (EMET)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "tassels [gedilim]" |
| LOGOS | "tassels" |
| PNEUMA | "tassels" |

**Ruling:** Consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (no bracket needed).

---

## v.14 — "tokens of virginity" (EMET) vs. "signs of virginity" (LOGOS) vs. "evidence of her virginity" / "she was not a virgin" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the tokens of virginity [betulim]" |
| LOGOS | "the signs of virginity" |
| PNEUMA | "she was not a virgin" / "the evidence of my daughter's virginity" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *betulim* = "signs/tokens of virginity." EMET: "tokens." LOGOS: "signs." PNEUMA renders the speech paraphrastically ("she was not a virgin" instead of "I did not find the tokens/signs"). Consensus follows LOGOS ("signs of virginity") — more natural than "tokens" and more precise than PNEUMA's paraphrase.

---

## v.17 — "tokens" vs. "signs" vs. "evidence"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Yet these are the tokens of my daughter's virginity" |
| LOGOS | "And yet these are the signs of my daughter's virginity" |
| PNEUMA | "but here is the evidence of my daughter's virginity" |

**Ruling:** LOGOS: "signs." EMET: "tokens." PNEUMA: "evidence." Consensus follows LOGOS ("signs") for consistency with v.14.

---

## v.17 — "disgraceful things" (EMET) vs. "shameful conduct" (LOGOS) vs. "slanderous charges" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "he has placed [a charge of] disgraceful things" |
| LOGOS | "he has charged her with shameful conduct" |
| PNEUMA | "he has made slanderous charges" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *alilot devarim* = "acts/charges of things" — a charge of improper conduct. LOGOS: "charged her with shameful conduct." PNEUMA: "slanderous charges" (adds "slanderous" from the false nature of the charge). Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.21 — "practicing prostitution" (LOGOS) vs. "commit prostitution" (EMET) vs. "sleeping around" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "to commit prostitution in her father's house" |
| LOGOS | "by practicing prostitution in her father's house" |
| PNEUMA | "by sleeping around while still in her father's house" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *liznot* (Qal inf. of *zanah*). In this context = pre-marital sexual activity. EMET/LOGOS: "prostitution." PNEUMA: "sleeping around" (too colloquial). Consensus: "practicing sexual immorality" — *zanah* here denotes sexual promiscuity rather than commercial prostitution specifically; "sexual immorality" is more accurate to the legal context.

---

## v.22 — "wife of another man" (LOGOS) vs. "wife of a husband [ba'alat ba'al]" (EMET) vs. "another man's wife" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "a woman who is a wife of a husband [ba'alat ba'al]" |
| LOGOS | "a woman who is the wife of another man" |
| PNEUMA | "another man's wife" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *ba'alat ba'al* = "mistress of a master/husband." EMET preserves the doubled root with bracket. LOGOS: "wife of another man." PNEUMA: "another man's wife" (most compact). Consensus follows LOGOS for the fuller formulation.

---

## v.24 — "violated his neighbor's wife" (LOGOS) vs. "humbled his neighbor's wife" (EMET) vs. "violated another man's wife" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "he humbled his neighbor's wife" |
| LOGOS | "he violated his neighbor's wife" |
| PNEUMA | "he violated another man's wife" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *inna et eshet re'ehu* = "he humbled/violated his neighbor's wife." The Pi'el *anah* here implies coercion/violation. "Violated" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) better conveys the offense. Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## vv.25, 28 — "seizes her" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "rapes her" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "seizes her and lies with her" |
| LOGOS | "seizes her and lies with her" |
| PNEUMA | "rapes her" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *chazaq* (v.25) / *taphas* (v.28) + *shakhav*. EMET/LOGOS: "seizes her and lies with her" — the two verbs are preserved, and the coercive nature is clear from context. PNEUMA: "rapes her" — accurate interpretation but collapses two verbs into one English word. Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS, letting the explicit acquittal of the woman (v.26) carry the interpretive weight.

---

## v.30 — "uncover his father's skirt" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "dishonor his father's bed" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "shall not uncover his father's skirt" |
| LOGOS | "so that he does not uncover his father's skirt" |
| PNEUMA | "he must not dishonor his father's bed" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *lo yegaleh kenaf aviv* = "he shall not uncover the skirt/wing of his father." EMET/LOGOS: literal euphemism preserved. PNEUMA: paraphrase. Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

*SOPHIA — Deuteronomy 22 disagreements logged: 18*
